_meta.description: TheHive 5 case template for SSH/RDP brute force incidents
_meta.author: SOC Lab Project

name: Brute Force Attack
description:
## Brute Force Authentication Attack

This case is opened when Wazuh detects 10 or more failed authentication attempts from a single source IP within a 60-second window, with optional correlation to a subsequent successful login.

### Severity Guide
- **Low**: Failed attempts only, source is known scanner (Shodan, Censys)
- **Medium**: High volume of failures, unknown source
- **High**: Failures followed by successful login
- **Critical**: Successful login + lateral movement indicators

### Key Questions
1. Did the brute force succeed (rule 100002 fired)?
2. Is the source IP associated with known threat actors?
3. Was the targeted account privileged?
4. Were any commands run post-authentication?

tasks.title: TRIAGE — Validate Alert
tasks.description:
## Triage Steps

1. Open Wazuh Dashboard — confirm rule 100001 or 100002 fired
2. Note the source IP, targeted username, and timestamp
3. Check if rule 100002 (success after brute force) also fired
4. Query Kibana saved search: `[SOC] Failed + Successful Logins by Source IP`
5. Confirm this is NOT a known scanner (check GreyNoise / Shodan)
6. Set case severity based on severity guide in description
7. Assign case to analyst

**Expected output:** Confirmed IP, targeted account, success/failure status

tasks.title: CONTAINMENT — Block Source IP
tasks.description:
## Containment Steps

1. Confirm the attacker IP from Wazuh alert field `data.srcip`
2. Check if IP is still actively attacking:
   ```bash
   grep <attacker_ip> /var/log/auth.log | tail -20
   ```
3. Block IP at host firewall:
   ```bash
   sudo ufw deny from <attacker_ip> to any
   # OR for iptables:
   sudo iptables -I INPUT -s <attacker_ip> -j DROP
   ```
4. If brute force succeeded — isolate compromised host from network
5. Document block action with timestamp and rule used
6. Verify block is effective:
   ```bash
   sudo ufw status numbered
   ```

**Expected output:** IP blocked, logged, and verified

tasks.title: INVESTIGATION — Analyse Login Activity
tasks.description:
## Investigation Steps

1. If login succeeded, check what the attacker did:
   ```bash
   # Review auth log for the compromised account
   grep <username> /var/log/auth.log
   
   # Check bash history
   cat /home/<username>/.bash_history
   cat /root/.bash_history
   
   # Check for new files
   find / -newer /tmp -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc
   
   # Check active sessions
   who -a
   last -n 30
   ```
2. Check for privilege escalation post-login:
   ```bash
   grep sudo /var/log/auth.log | grep <username>
   ```
3. Check for lateral movement (new outbound connections):
   ```bash
   ss -tulpn
   netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED
   ```
4. Correlate in Kibana — search by `data.srcip` across all rule groups
5. GeoIP lookup the attacker IP
6. Check IP against threat intel (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB)

**Expected output:** Full timeline of attacker activity if login succeeded

tasks.title: ERADICATION — Remove Attacker Foothold
tasks.description:
## Eradication Steps (if login succeeded)

1. Lock or disable the compromised account:
   ```bash
   sudo passwd -l <username>
   # OR
   sudo usermod --expiredate 1 <username>
   ```
2. Rotate SSH keys for the affected account:
   ```bash
   rm /home/<username>/.ssh/authorized_keys
   ```
3. Check for persistence mechanisms:
   ```bash
   crontab -l -u <username>
   cat /etc/cron.d/*
   ls /etc/cron.daily/ /etc/cron.hourly/
   grep <username> /etc/passwd
   ls -la /home/<username>/.bashrc /home/<username>/.profile
   ```
4. Remove any unauthorized accounts created:
   ```bash
   awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd
   ```
5. Scan for backdoors / webshells:
   ```bash
   find /var/www /tmp /dev/shm -name '*.php' -o -name '*.sh' 2>/dev/null
   ```

**Expected output:** Attacker access revoked, persistence removed

tasks.title: RECOVERY — Restore & Harden
tasks.description:
## Recovery Steps

1. Re-enable account after password reset (if legitimate user):
   ```bash
   sudo passwd <username>   # Set strong password
   sudo passwd -u <username> # Unlock account
   ```
2. Harden SSH configuration:
   ```bash
   sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
   # Set:
   #   PasswordAuthentication no
   #   PermitRootLogin no
   #   MaxAuthTries 3
   #   AllowUsers <specific_users>
   sudo systemctl restart sshd
   ```
3. Install / configure fail2ban if not present:
   ```bash
   sudo apt install fail2ban -y
   sudo systemctl enable --now fail2ban
   ```
4. Verify Wazuh active-response is configured to auto-block repeat offenders
5. Confirm monitoring is restored and alerting works

**Expected output:** Service hardened, monitoring confirmed

tasks.title: POST-INCIDENT — Document & Report
tasks.description:
## Post-Incident Steps

1. Complete timeline in case description
2. Fill in all custom fields (source IP, targeted account, outcome)
3. Export Wazuh alert data as evidence attachment
4. Write lessons learned:
   - What detection worked?
   - What could be improved?
   - Were thresholds appropriate?
5. Update IP blocklist / threat intel feed
6. File formal incident report using IR-001 template
7. Close case with resolution summary

**Expected output:** Case closed, report filed, improvements logged

customFields.name: attackerIP
customFields.description: Source IP of the brute force attack

customFields.name: targetedHost
customFields.description: Hostname or IP of the attacked system

customFields.name: wazuhRuleID
customFields.description: Wazuh rule ID that triggered this case

customFields.name: failedAttemptCount
customFields.description: Total number of failed login attempts
